9: kekol asher ani mareeh otcha et tabnit hamishkan veet tabnit kol kelav veken taashu: 'Exactly as I show you concerning the pattern [tabnit] of the tabernacle, and of all its furniture, so you shall make it.' The Hebrew tabnit (pattern/model) implies the earthly tabernacle is a copy of a heavenly original. Hebrews (8:5) quotes this verse explicitly (using LXX typos — pattern, type) to argue that the Levitical priests serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things. The tabernacle's typological function is not a later Christian imposition but is built into the original instructions: Moses saw the heavenly original; Israel built the earthly copy.
1: Standard commission formula: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe lemor (vay-yeh-dah-BER YHWH el-Mo-SHEH le-MOR): 'And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying.' The Sinai legislation now pivots from the Decalogue and mishpatim (civil law, chs. 21-23) to the construction of the divine dwelling — miqdash and mishkan — as the covenant's physical expression.
2: terumah (teh-roo-MAH): 'contribution/heave offering.' H8641, from rum (to lift/raise — H7311). The offering is lifted toward God. Critical qualifier: nediv libbo (neh-DEEV lib-BO), 'whose heart moves him willingly' — H5068 from nadav (to give freely/volunteer). The tabernacle is built by freewill generosity, not conscription. Same root as the nedivim (noble/generous ones) throughout the Psalms. Paul echoes this principle in 2 Cor 9:7.
3: Materials in descending value: zahav, kesef, nechoshet (zah-HAV, KEH-sef, neh-KHO-shet): 'gold, silver, bronze.' These three metals map spatially onto the tabernacle zones — gold in the most holy place and holy place, silver as socket-foundations, bronze in the outer court. Sacred space is marked materially: proximity to divine presence corresponds to purity of metal.
4: tekhelet (teh-kheh-LET): 'blue' (H8504, possibly from Murex snail dye — same source as the tzitzit blue thread, Num 15:38). argaman (ar-gah-MAN): 'purple' (H713, a prestige color in the ancient world, associated with royalty). tola'at shani (toh-lah-AT shah-NEE): 'scarlet worm-dye' (H8438/H8144, from the kermes scale insect). shesh (shesh): 'fine linen' (H8336, Egyptian). These four materials are the signature of sacred space throughout the tabernacle, priestly garments, and the high priest's vestments — a visual theology of holiness.
5: ore tachash (oh-ROT tah-KHASH): 'tachash skins' (H8476 — the animal is unidentified; candidates include dugong, porpoise, or a now-extinct land animal). atzei shittim (ah-TZEI shit-TEEM): 'acacia wood' (H7848 H6086). Acacia is a desert-resistant hardwood — extremely dense, resistant to rot and insects. Its use for the ark, altar, and table is theologically significant: the incorruptible wood that bears the holy furnishings anticipates the incorruptible flesh that bears the divine presence (cf. Ps 16:10, Acts 2:27).
6: shemen hammishchah (sheh-MEN ha-MISH-chah): 'the anointing oil' (H8081 H4888, from mashach — to anoint, the root of mashiach/Messiah). qetoret hasamim (qeh-TOH-ret ha-sam-MEEM): 'incense of spices' (H7004 H1314). Both items define the priestly/cultic ministry: anointing consecrates, incense represents the ascending prayers of the people before God (cf. Rev 8:3-4).
7: avnei shoham (av-NEI sho-HAM): 'shoham stones' (H7718 — possibly onyx or a precious stone; identification uncertain). avnei milu'im (av-NEI mih-loo-EEM): 'setting/filling stones' (H4394, from male H4390 — to fill). The word for gem settings (milu) echoes the filling/fulfillment language of creation (Gen 1:28 umilu) — the sacred garments are filled/completed with precious stones.
8: miqdash (mik-DASH): 'sanctuary' (H4720, from qadash — to be holy/set apart). mishkan (mish-KAN): 'tabernacle/dwelling' (H4908, from shakan H7931 — to dwell/settle/tabernacle). The purpose statement — veshakhanti betokham, 'that I may dwell among them' — is one of the most theologically loaded sentences in the Torah. The mission of the tabernacle is divine indwelling in the midst of the people. John 1:14 employs the same Greek root (eskenos = tabernacled) to describe the Incarnation: the eternal Word pitched his tabernacle among us.
10: aron (ah-RON): 'ark/chest/box' (H727). The same word is used for Joseph's coffin in Gen 50:26 — the only two uses in the Pentateuch of aron for a chest. The ark contains the tablets of the covenant — it is the treasure chest of the divine word. Dimensions: 2.5 cubits long, 1.5 wide, 1.5 high. atzei shittim — acacia wood again — the incorruptible wood at the heart of the sanctuary.
11: zahav tahor (zah-HAV tah-HOR): 'pure gold' (H2091 H2889). The ark is overlaid with pure gold inside and out — total consecration, every surface. zar zahav (zehr zah-HAV): 'crown/molding of gold' (H2213), surrounding the top of the ark — a royal crown befitting the footstool of the King of the universe.
12: arba tabbaot zahav (ar-BAH tab-ah-OT zah-HAV): 'four gold rings' (H702 H2885 H2091). The rings are the interface between the holy ark and the carrying poles — the only means by which human hands can transport the divine presence without touching it directly. Placed at the four feet, or at the four lower corners, of the ark.
13: baddim (bad-DEEM): 'poles/staves' (H905, plural of bad). The root bad can mean separation, isolation, or the pole itself. The carrying poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold are the instruments by which the presence of God travels with the people. The baddim make the ark portable — YHWH is not bound to a fixed place but travels with his covenant community.
14: The poles are inserted into the rings on the sides of the ark — the ark's carrying system. The instruction anticipates the wilderness journey: the divine presence must move with Israel through the desert. The danger of touching the ark directly is illustrated later in the narrative of Uzzah (2 Sam 6:6-7), where even well-intentioned contact without the prescribed mediation proves fatal — holiness requires mediation.
15: lo yasuru mimmenu (lo yah-SOO-roo mim-ME-noo): 'they shall not be removed from it.' The poles are permanently in place — the ark is always in a state of readiness for movement. This permanence of the carrying apparatus reflects the pilgrim nature of the divine dwelling: even when Israel is at rest, God's presence is ready to move. The command distinguishes the ark from other sacred furniture whose poles could be removed.
16: haedut (hah-ay-DOOT): 'the testimony/witness tablets' (H5715, from ud — to testify/repeat). The tablets of the Decalogue placed inside the ark are haedut — the divine testimony, the witness of covenant. This names the ark: aron haedut = ark of the testimony. The tablets inside the ark function as the covenant document at the heart of the divine dwelling, like a suzerainty treaty stored in the sanctuary.
17: kapporet (kap-PO-ret): 'atonement cover/mercy seat' (H3727, from kapar H3722 — to cover, to atone, to make propitiation). This is the most theologically concentrated piece of furniture in the tabernacle. On Yom Kippur (Lev 16), the high priest sprinkles the blood of the sin offering on the kapporet — the blood of atonement applied directly to the cover of the covenant tablets, between the cherubim, in the divine presence. The LXX renders it hilasterion, the same word Paul uses in Rom 3:25 for what God put forward in Christ as the place of atonement. Dimensions: 2.5 cubits × 1.5 cubits — exact match to the top of the ark.
18: shenei cheruvim zahav (she-NEI kheh-roo-VEEM zah-HAV): 'two gold cherubim.' mikshah (mik-SHAH): 'hammered/beaten work' (H4749, from naqash? or kasha = beaten into shape). The cherubim are not cast separately and mounted — they are beaten out of the same gold as the kapporet. Cherubim (H3742) are the guardians of sacred divine space throughout Scripture, from Eden (Gen 3:24) to the heavenly throne room (Ezek 1, Rev 4).
19: miqqetseh hakapporet (mik-KET-zeh hah-kap-PO-ret): 'from the end of the atonement cover.' The two cherubim emerge from the two ends of the kapporet — they are one piece with it. The divine meeting place is flanked by the guardians of divine holiness. The unity of material (one piece of hammered gold) signals that access to the divine presence is inseparable from the atoning mediation of the kapporet.
20: porsim kanfeihem (po-REEM kan-fei-HEM): 'spreading their wings' (H6566/H3671, paras/kanaph). The wings of the cherubim shelter or overshadow the kapporet from above. upeneihem ish el achiv: 'their faces toward one another' — facing each other, looking down toward the kapporet. This posture defines the divine throne: YHWH is enthroned above the cherubim (1 Sam 4:4, Ps 80:1), in the space between their overshadowing wings.
21: The kapporet is placed on top of the ark, with the edut (testimony tablets) inside. The sequence — covenant document inside, atonement cover on top — is architecturally theological: the covenant law is covered by (and accessible only through) the atonement of divine mercy. The tablets demand; the kapporet answers.
22: veno'adti lekha sham (ve-no-AD-ti lekh-AH SHAM): 'and I will meet with you there' (H3259, from ya'ad — to appoint, to meet at an appointed time). The word for meeting (mo'ed) comes from this root — the Tent of Meeting (ohel mo'ed) is the place of divine appointments with Israel. mi'bayn shnei hakeruvim: 'from between the two cherubim' — the precise location of YHWH's speech: the mercy seat between the cherubim is the interface between heaven and earth, the place where divine communication enters human history.
23: shulhan (shool-KHAN): 'table' (H7979). The table for the bread of the presence stands in the holy place outside the veil. Acacia wood, overlaid with gold. The table is the second piece of holy furniture — after the ark, the table. Israel's covenant with YHWH is structured as a meal before him.
24: Pure gold overlay for the table; zar zahav (crown/molding of gold) surrounding it — matching the ark's crown. The gold crown or rim may have served a practical function (keeping items in place) while symbolically marking the table as royal furnishing in the divine throne room.
25: misgereth (mis-GEH-ret): 'border/frame/rim' (H4526, from sagar — to close/shut H5462). A handsbreadth-wide border surrounding the table. zar zahav again — the crown molding appears on both the ark and the table, marking them as the two central furnishings of the divine dwelling (covenant and provision).
26: Four gold rings for the table, one at each corner — the same carrying system as the ark. The rings placed at the four feet enable the table to travel with Israel. The table of the divine presence is not a fixed monument but a traveling provision for the pilgrim people.
27: The rings are close to the misgereth (border/rim) to hold the carrying poles. Precise engineering — the rings must be positioned to keep the poles level with the table surface during transport, preventing the sacred bread and vessels from sliding.
28: baddei shittim (bad-DEI shit-TEEM): 'acacia poles' — same material and principle as the ark's poles. The table travels on the same wood as the ark: acacia (incorruptible desert hardwood) overlaid with gold. The table is as portable as the covenant — divine provision travels wherever the covenant people travel.
29: qe'arotav (ke-ah-ro-TAV): 'its plates' (H7086); kappotav (kap-po-TAV): 'its pans/dishes' (H3709 — same word as palms of hands, the cupped shape); qeshavot (ke-sha-VOT): 'its pitchers/flagons' (H7184); menaqiyyot (me-nah-ki-YOT): 'its bowls' (H4518). All for the nesekh (NEH-sekh): 'libation/drink offering' (H5262). The table is fully equipped for a sacred meal service — vessels for bread, wine, and offering. Divine hospitality is not sparse.
30: lehem hapanim (LEH-khem hah-pah-NEEM): 'bread of the face/presence' (H3899 H6440). Literally: face-bread, or presence-bread — twelve loaves set before the face of YHWH perpetually (tamid). The twelve loaves represent the twelve tribes in constant presentation before God. In 1 Sam 21:1-6 David eats the sacred showbread in extremity, and Jesus appeals to this incident to teach that the sabbath/cultic law serves human need (Matt 12:3-4). The lehem hapanim anticipates the eschatological banquet where the redeemed eat at the divine table.
31: menorah (me-no-RAH): 'lampstand' (H4501, from root nwr/nyr — light, lamp H5216). zahav tahor — pure gold. mikshah: hammered out of one piece — not assembled from parts but beaten into its branching form from a single talent of pure gold. The menorah is the light of the divine house. Zechariah 4 interprets the menorah as the light of YHWH's Spirit (not by might nor by power). John 8:12 applies the light-language to Jesus as the light of the world.
32: shisha qanim (shee-SHA kah-NEEM): 'six branches' (H8337 H7070) — three from each side of the central shaft. yerech (YEH-rekh): 'thigh/central shaft' (H3409 — the same word used for the thigh/base of the body). The lamp's central pillar is described with the human body word for the thigh — architectural anthropomorphism, the living lamp with a spine and branches.
33: gevi'im meshukadim (ge-vi-EEM me-shoo-KAH-deem): 'almond-shaped cups' (H1375 H8247). The menorah's ornamental cups are shaped like almond blossoms — the shaqed (almond tree H8247) is the first tree to blossom in Israel (January), its name from shaqad = to wake/be alert/watch. Aaron's rod that budded was an almond branch (Num 17:8). kaftor (kaf-TOR): 'knob/capital' (H3730); perach (PEH-rakh): 'flower/blossom' (H6525). The lamp is adorned with imagery of new life, watchfulness, and blossoming.
34: The central shaft has four cups with knobs and blossoms — more ornamentation than the six branches (which have three each). The central trunk of the menorah is the most elaborate — the source from which the branches radiate. This structural emphasis on the central shaft anticipates the menorah's interpretation in Zechariah 4 and John 15 (the vine and branches).
35: The structural specification: a knob beneath each pair of branches — there are three pairs of branches, so three knobs on the central shaft where branches emerge. The total structure has a mathematical harmony: 7 lamps, 6 branches, specific cup/knob/blossom counts at each joint. This precision is not mere aesthetics but reflects the orderliness of creation and divine wisdom.
36: mikshah echad zahav tahor (mik-SHAH eh-KHAD zah-HAV tah-HOR): 'one hammered piece of pure gold.' The entire menorah — shaft, branches, cups, knobs, blossoms — is beaten from one talent of gold. No joints, no assembly, no seams. This unity of material reflects the oneness of the divine light that illuminates Israel. The menorah cannot be disassembled into parts; its light is whole and indivisible.
37: shiv'at neroteyha (shiv-AT ne-ro-TEI-hah): 'its seven lamps' (H7651 H5216). Seven is the number of covenantal completeness throughout Scripture. The seven lamps are to be arranged to illuminate the space in front of the lampstand — el ever paneyha: toward its face. The menorah faces the table of the bread of the presence — the light illuminates the divine provision, and both face the entrance, available to approach.
38: melqachayyim (mel-kah-KHI-yim): 'tongs/snuffers' (H4457, from laqach — to take); machtot (makh-TOT): 'trays/fire pans' (H4289). All pure gold. The tongs for trimming the wicks and the trays for carrying the ash are as sacred as the lamp itself — every element of worship is consecrated. Even the maintenance tools bear the mark of holiness.
39: kikar zahav tahor (ki-KAR zah-HAV tah-HOR): 'a talent of pure gold' (H3603 H2091 H2889). A kikar is approximately 34 kg / 75 lbs. The entire menorah, including all its vessels, is hammered from one talent of pure gold. This is one of the largest single-material sacred objects — the light of the divine house is costly, the most expensive furnishing in terms of raw gold weight.
40: ure'eh va'aseh betavnitam (oo-re-EH va-ah-SEH be-tav-ni-TAM): 'and see and make according to their pattern' (H7200/H6213/H8403). The closing command echoes v.9 — both the tabernacle and the menorah are built from the tabnit revealed on the mountain. asher-attah mar'eh bahar: 'which you are shown on the mountain.' Heb 8:5 and 9:23 apply this principle broadly: the entire Levitical system is a copy (hupodeigma) and shadow (skia) of the heavenly realities. The earthly tabernacle does not merely house God — it images the heavenly sanctuary where Christ now ministers.
